For people loosing their minds at 1:44:59 as to why it refuses to work, mongo-express changed how you pass the server URL now (without updating the docker hub page docs for at least 2 years now!!!), so it's unable to find your mongodb instance. The new way basically makes it impossible to use the secret and configmap variables to dynamically define the URL, at least to my knowledge.

What you need to do is add a new environmental variable:
          - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_URL
            value: "mongodb://<<the username you used (plaintext, not base64)>>:<<the password you used>>@<<mongodb service name>>:<<port>>"

If you followed the guide, this would be:
            value: "mongodb://username:password@mongodb-service:27017"


From what I see, ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINPASSWORD, ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINUSERNAME, and ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_SERVER don't do anything anymore. I'm not sure if there is a way to still use kubernetes secrets or configmaps to do this anymore, since you can't combine them together in the same line.

If you're having a problem with the db here is a solution I found. You have to set the mongo url env to be able to connect properly, also notice that in the video the red text indicates, the actual credentials that you need to log into the Mongo Express dashboard are admin:pass, for this you also need to set the basicauth username and password not only admin. Here is the full mongo-express.yaml file:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mongo-express
  labels:
    app: mongo-express
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mongo-express
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mongo-express
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: mongo-express
        image: mongo-express
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8081
        env:
        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ENABLE_ADMIN
          value: 'true'
        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINUSERNAME
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: mongodb-secret
              key: mongo-root-username
        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINPASSWORD
          valueFrom: 
            secretKeyRef:
              name: mongodb-secret
              key: mongo-root-password
        - name: ME_CONFIG_BASICAUTH_USERNAME
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: mongodb-secret
              key: mongo-root-username
        - name: ME_CONFIG_BASICAUTH_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: mongodb-secret
              key: mongo-root-password
        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_SERVER
          valueFrom: 
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: mongodb-configmap
              key: database_url
        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_URL
          value: "mongodb://$(ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINUSERNAME):$(ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINPASSWORD)@mongodb-service:27017/"

at 02:13:12 ....

to view a list of addons available: minikube addons list
to enable: minikube addons enable ingress (do the list again to see a check mark on this in the list)
to show available namespaces: kubectl get namespace (look for ingress-nginx)

i could not find "nginx-ingress-controller" on my minikube.
instead, i had to look up using

kubectl get pod -n ingress-nginx
---> shows

ingress-nginx-admission-create-hash1
ingress-nginx-admission-patch-hash2
ingress-nginx-controller-hash3  (Running)

02:13:30  kubernetes dashboard was not started on my minikube!!

for this, 
minikube addons enable metrics-server
minikube dashboard

(the default browser would show the kubernetes dashboard)


02:13:38   kubectl get namespace
would now show kubernetes-dashboard(ACTIVE)

to get the components under this namespace, 
kubectl get all -n kubernetes-dashboard

-->
pod/dashboard-metrics-scraper-hash(Running)
pod/kubernetes-dashboard-hash_another(Running)

and services, replicaset and deployment.

Great Video (my second time watching).  I think you made a mistake with the targetPort/port in the example starting around 1:02:03 

The nginx container listens on port 80 by default.  So, in the deployment the containerPort should be 80.  In the service it should map inbound port 8080 to container/pod port 80.  So the service targetPort would be 80, and the port would be 8080.  When I configure it this way I can spin up another pod in the same cluster and then curl http://<service CLUSTER-IP>:8080 with success.

If the nginx process in the container/pod was changed to listen on port 8080, then the way you describe would work (curl http://<service CLUSTERIP>:80).

Please let me know if I'm misunderstanding.  I feel like I'm finally starting to understand this stuff thanks to your video.

For Windows users:

1. Install Docker desktop and use "minikube start --vm-driver=docker" (instead of hyperkit)
2. In order to access mongo-express-service: 
    - Open a new terminal window and run: "minikube tunnel" (gives the service an External IP, do not close this window)
    - Run in another terminal: "kubectl get service" (to see what External IP got applied)
    - Open a browser and use the External IP and the 8081 port instead of the 30000 port (example: "127.0.0.1:8081")
    - FYI: as of the current release of mongo-express you can't see the server stats. This seems to have been fixed for future release though.

Hope that helps :)

It is a great video, thank you for that. However, I need to touch on one point in "K8s YAML Configuration File" part. There is a wrong explanation if I am not mistaken about the part that in service.yaml file, "app:nginx" at spec.selector has nothing to do with "app:nginx" at metadata.labels in deployment.yaml file. It is only connected to the spec.template.metadata.labels because it is the label of pods (2 replicas), not the label of deployment. Using same label name for both deployment and pod creates confusion. "app:nginx" in spec.matchLabels is enough to connect pods to the deployment. Deployment label in this example is optional I suppose.

One thing caught me out - as I had been playing around with minikube before coming across this tutorial, I had already set up a production and development namespace. When it came to trying to connect to mongo-express I was getting "[SERVICE_NOT_FOUND] Could not find finalized endpoint being pointed to by mongo-express-service". For anyone like me who comes across this, the 'fix' was to specify the namespace (minikube doesn't seem to know about the namespace current context even if it is set in your environment).  Just use "minikube service mongo-express-service -n <whatever_your_namespace>".

One thing I would add - I was coming across an error which didnt authorise my database access (at 1:45 (1 hour 45 mins)) .

 When checking the mongo-express pod logs, i'd get the following error:

 Error [MongoError]: command listDatabases requires authentication. 

If you find this error, make sure you have the database username and passwords referenced in the mongo-express deployment. These are ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINUSERNAME and ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINPASSWORD. 

The ones which are in the deployment file for mongo-express in the video are ME_CONFIG_BASICAUTH_USERNAME and ME_CONFIG_BASICAUTH_PASSWORD, which if you see in the descriptions column in https://hub.docker.com/_/mongo-express?tab=description&page=1&ordering=last_updated, you'll see they're the auths for accessing just the web api.

Hi Nana. (33:00) For multi-master clusters, isn't it quite dangerous to have 2 or even number of master nodes? The reason is, if a master node breaks down, the remaining master nodes can have a new leader, but the minority node(s) that got disconnected or broke down cannot be a leader anymore. So, if 1 master node breaks down in a 2 master node set-up, the remaining node cannot determine that it is in the majority. So, an etcd leader can never be elected. Same thing with 4 master nodes, and 2 nodes break down, the remaining nodes cannot be a majority, so the entire cluster goes down. Therefore, the number of master nodes should always be odd number. 3 master nodes can survive 1 master node break down. 5 master nodes can survive 2 nodes breakdown. 7 master nodes can survive a 3 node breakdown. 1 master node is safer than 2 master nodes. 3 multi master nodes is a good setup. 5 multi-master node is more than enough. 7 multi-master nodes is somewhat the maximum.

Nana I want to make a useful suggestion for the part titled "K8s YAML Configuration File". In all the books that I have looked, the same bad habit is present, and you also have it in your video (your video is  generally is very good , on most aspects). You have the app: nginx , and  in the specification of the container you have name:nginx . For this simple case it may not be harmful and does not make things difficult to understand , but there are so many cases that this habit of using the same name for different objects can obscure understanding that every time I see it I want to scream! So it would be perfect if you had 
name:nginx-cont
as name for the container. 
Generally remember that practice for your future videos!

#yaml file for version 1.20
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: dashboard-ingress
  namespace: kubernetes-dashboard
spec:
  rules:
    - host: "dashboard.com"
      http:
        paths: 
        - pathType: Prefix
          path: "/"
          backend:
            service:
              name: kubernetes-dashboard
              port: 
                number: 80

I am currently halfway through and I have encountered first problem with your example in 🔥  K8s Ingress explained 🔥, which is actually due to breaking changes in Ingress Kubernetes v1.19
The ingress configuration syntax received many changes and previous syntax is no longer valid. Leaving my solution below for other lost learners.

Docs for Ingress on v1.19: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/ 

Currently valid configuration for people using v1.19+ is:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: dashboard-ingress
  namespace: kubernetes-dashboard
spec:
  rules:
  - host: dashboard.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: kubernetes-dashboard
            port:
              number: 80

If anyone experiences a minikube memory issue "Exiting due to RSRC_INSUFFICIENT_REQ_MEMORY: Requested memory allocation 0MiB is less than the usable minimum of 953MB". You need to run `minikube config set disk-size 2000mb` then you need to delete the minikube as follows: `minikube delete` and then `minikube start`. At least this worked for me... all the best

I'm just about half way through and it's been really easy to follow and code along - well done :)

But there are some new developments - the Ingress YAML at about 2h 17m does not work as downloaded from gitLab.
The API v1beta1 Ingress is deprecated in v1.14+, and will become unavailable in v1.22+ so I looked up in the documentation and 
modified it accordingly to the new V1 schema:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: dashboard-ingress
  namespace: kubernetes-dashboard
spec:
  rules:
  - host: dashboard.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: kubernetes-dashboard
            port:
              number: 80

Also, the kubernetes-dashboard namespace and all related things are not present by default ATM, you have to start it manually with "minikube dashboard" and that triggers the creation of the namespace and so on.

As a penetration tester, I want to clarify what was said about Kubernetes secrets. One of the common things we find we conducting security assessments is developers thinking that Base64 is a form of encryption. Base64 is NOT a form of encryption, and should be considered the same as plaintext. Kubernetes Secrets provide protections around who can access secrets, but the secrets themselves are not encrypted and are easily reversible when specified as a Base64 string through a manifest. Base64 is only a different form of storing data, and should NEVER be considered as offering any form of security.

See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#security-properties for more information.

brew install bash-completion
then you enable kubernetes completion via adding this to your ~/.bashrc:
source <(kubectl completion bash)
and then you need to restart ~/.bashrc:
source ~/.bashrc
